narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, a middle-income family background, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 28, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 44.2%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking in the bottom group, well below the typical student's mid-range position, signaling to the model a concerning dip in academic performance over time. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA played the strongest role in countering that risk, as your 2.80 is close to the average and suggested some foundational strength. Your LSAT score, though lower than the dataset average of around 37, also helped pull the prediction toward passing by demonstrating potential in a key standardized measure. Being full-time further nudged things positively, aligning with the vast majority of successful students who follow this path. Less impactful but still noted were your first-year decile, also low, and middle family income, both slightly tilting toward higher risk compared to averages. Overall, while some elements like your GPA and LSAT showed promise relative to peers, the model weighed your weaker school deciles—especially in year three—as the dominant concern, setting you apart from typical admitted students with steadier academic trajectories. This isn't a reflection of your worth or potential, but a data-driven call on bar exam readiness. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that address these key areas—we believe in your ability to turn this around.
